claim_reviews.claim: We computed the complete Kronecker coefficient table for all triples of partitions of n = 20 in 3.7 seconds and n = 30 in 7.4 minutes on a single NVIDIA B200 GPU.
claim_reviews.evidence: For n=30 the total arithmetic work is roughly p(30)^4 ≈ 1.46×10^14 multiply-adds when implemented as a conjugacy-class dot product. Sustaining 3.3×10^11 op/s over 445 s is within the published 8-bit / 16-bit integer throughput of the B200 (~2 PFLOP), and the authors avoid memory blow-up by reducing in-kernel, so the reported wall-times are physically reasonable.

claim_reviews.claim: The S30 table contains 29.3 billion unordered triples (26.4 billion non-zero, 89.9 % density) and the maximal coefficient is 24.2 trillion.
claim_reviews.evidence: 29.3 billion equals C(p(30)+2,3) with p(30)=5 604, confirming the counting. Density and max value have not been independently checked; the public HF dump is too large to spot-check manually in the paper.
claim_reviews.concerns: Provide a checksum or small random sample so reviewers can cross–validate the large dataset without downloading hundreds of GB.

claim_reviews.claim: This is the largest Kronecker coefficient computation ever published; the previous systematic frontier was n≈20.
claim_reviews.evidence: The biggest previously documented exhaustive tables appear to be n≤25 (Bürgisser & Ikenmeyer 2008; online packages). A literature search (zbMATH, arXiv) finds no peer-reviewed work beyond n=25, but the claim would benefit from explicit citation of the current record.
claim_reviews.concerns: Cite the highest-n full table in the literature or clarify that no such citation exists.

claim_reviews.claim: Character tables were computed via the Murnaghan-Nakayama rule and validated by row/column orthogonality and the dimension sum formula.
claim_reviews.evidence: Orthogonality and ∑dim²=n! are standard, but the manuscript states these tests were executed only up to n=12. No evidence is given that the n=30 table itself passed the same checks, although this is straightforward to perform.
claim_reviews.concerns: Include explicit verification results (max absolute orthogonality error, integer overflow safeguards) for n=20 and n=30.

claim_reviews.claim: The GPU method (5 604 slabs × 31 M threads) can perform the required ~1.5×10^14 operations in 445 s on a B200.
claim_reviews.evidence: Operation count and throughput analysis (see first claim) match the hardware spec; memory footprint is kept constant by in-kernel reduction, so no architectural contradiction is evident.

claim_reviews.claim: The fraction of non-zero Kronecker triples rises from 79.5 % at n=20 to 89.9 % at n=30.
claim_reviews.evidence: Assuming the counts in claim 2 are correct, the percentages follow arithmetically. The trend is compatible with asymptotic results on saturation of the Kronecker cone (e.g., Ikenmeyer–Mulmuley–Walter 2017).

overall_assessment: The computation is mathematically standard and the performance claims are credible for a modern B200 GPU. However, validation of the n=30 character table and independent cross-checks of the huge output are missing, and the “largest ever” assertion lacks a direct literature comparison. These gaps do not undermine the technical achievement but should be remedied before archival publication.
recommendations: Publish orthogonality and dimension-sum residuals for n=20 and n=30 character tables.
Release a cryptographic hash and several random 10 000-triple subsets of the S30 output to enable lightweight verification.
Cite (or definitively rule out) the highest-n complete tables previously published to justify the novelty claim.
Document numerical precision (32-bit vs 64-bit) and overflow handling for coefficients up to 2.4×10^13.
certification_justification: The methods (Murnaghan-Nakayama, GPU tensor contraction) are well-established in at least two peer-reviewed sources, and performance is validated against hardware capabilities, but the work has not yet been benchmarked by three or more independent studies.
